data.title: Turkcell: Pain Today For More Prosperity Tomorrow
data.description:
I turned neutral on Turkcell (TKC) at the start of this year, concerned about management's strategic priorities, ongoing macroeconomic challenges in Türkiye, fierce competition in the Turkish mobile and fixed-line services markets, and the risk of meaningful capex spending increases.

Those issues have all come home to roost to varying degrees this year, leading the ADRs to a roughly 14% decline since my last update and underperformance relative to rivals like Turk Telekom (OTCPK:TRKNY) and Vodafone (VOD), other emerging market service providers like MTN Group (OTCPK:MTNOY), and the iShares MSCI Turkey ETF (TUR).

The reset in price and valuation makes this a more interesting situation, particularly in light of 2025, which has seen the company continue to maintain a leading share in Türkiye while generating better-than-expected margins and continuing to grow promising businesses like fiber services and mobile payments. The "but" is that the company is looking at major capex spending in the coming years to support a 5G rollout - a "pain today, prosper tomorrow" opportunity that will eventually improve profits and margins further.

Given how these capex overhangs have played out in the past, it seems early to call an "all clear", but it is a tempting name to monitor for further pullbacks.

A Better-Than-Expected Quarter

Turkcell continues to leverage best-in-class service quality and substantial investments in its mobile and fiber ecosystem to drive ongoing share growth in high-value postpaid subscribers while also maintaining ARPU growth comfortably ahead of Türkiye's still-problematic inflation. That, in turn, continues to drive some of the best reported margins the company has seen.

Revenue rose 11% in the third quarter, with core mobile service revenue up 11%. Consumer revenue (up about 11%) remains the large majority of the service business, but Corporate in seeing some decent growth (up 17%), helped by strong growth in data center and cloud services (up almost 51%).

Underpinning the service revenue growth, Turkcell saw modest overall subscriber growth (up less than 1% yoy and qoq), but 7% year-over-year growth in post-paid subs and strong growth in average revenue per user (or ARPU) of almost 10%, with adjusted post-paid ARPU growth of 11.5% (and these post-paid subs pay about 2.5x more on average per month than prepaid subs).

Turkcell also continues to see solid growth in its fiber business, with fiber subs up 4% yoy (to 2.5M versus 30.6M post-paid mobile subs) and residential fiber ARPU up 17%. At this point the main limiting factor in growing this business is access - the company's capture rate is over 42%, but they only pass around 6.2M homes at this point.

The company's tech-driven payments business continues to grow nicely as well, with overall Techfin revenue up 20% and Paycell revenue up 42% on 78% transaction volume growth.

EBITDA grew almost 11% this quarter, beating by more than 4% (as per Visible Alpha), with margin declining 30 bp to 43.9%. Turkcell Türkiye EBITDA rose 11% with margin up 10 bp to 45.7%, while Techfin saw EBITDA down 6% (margin down six points to 21.9%) due to a significant revenue mix shift toward lower-margin point-of-sale transactions as Türkiye's central bank previously okayed larger limits on mobile transactions.

With these results, management raised guidance for the remainder of the year, with revenue expected to grow 10% (versus 7% to 8% previously) and EBITDA margin of 42% to 43%.

Competition Has Been Rougher Of Late

While Turkcell's market share has been relatively stable in the low-40%s for quite some time, that may suggest a false impression of calmness in the market. In truth, Turk Telekom has been pushing hard to grow its business, outgrowing Turkcell in four of the last seven quarters (just missing another win in Q3), though with Turkcell typically enjoying better margins.

This competition has arguably put some limits on what Turkcell can do with pricing and has perhaps constrained subscriber growth somewhat. That said, it is Vodafone that has seen the bigger impact, with Turk Telekom passing Vodafone to become the #2 player with a 30% share (after quite a while in third place).

While the change in competition is more evident in the mobile services business, the fiber business has been highly competitive as well, with Turk Telekom enjoying a huge incumbent advantage in homes passed with its services (over 33 million, including close to 15 million with their most advanced fiber offerings).

I'm not too concerned about the longer-term ramifications here. Turk Telekom hasn't been behaving irresponsibly, and I don't see much risk of them "poisoning the well" for everybody. If anything, I think it's Vodafone that has the bigger issue now, as they seem less and less relevant in the post-paid space.

Spending Money To Make Money

The reasons why a stock has moved the way it has aren't always obvious, and I can appreciate that some investors may be frustrated by the dichotomy between Turkcell's strong reported operating performance and the share price performance.

The issue, I believe, is the impact of a major upcoming investment cycle.

Turkcell is moving forward with 5G, having won significant spectrum at auction in October and looking to launch in 2026. It's not as if the Street didn't know this was coming at some point, but now we know that the company will be paying $1.47B for 42% of the available 5G spectrum (the maximum it's allowed to own), with Turk Telekom getting around 35% and Vodafone getting the rest.

Although the 5G auctions were a known event, they brought in about 40% more than expected, and at least some analysts and investors are worried about the risk of "winner's curse" and Turkcell overpaying for capacity, not to mention the looming outlays for the actual 5G hardware. I'd also note that Turkcell extended its rights to 2G-4.5G spectrum through 2042, agreeing to pay 5% of gross mobile service revenue annually starting in 2029.

This isn't the first time this has happened; the market freaked out about the price Turkcell paid for 4G spectrum back in 2015, but I'd argue that those purchases have served the company well over the past decade as the company's leading 4G/4.5G offerings have made a significant difference in growing ARPU and facilitating ventures like mobile payments.

To be sure, capex spending will accelerate in the coming years, and I expect there will be concerns about whether or not the company can and will maintain (let alone grow) its dividend during that period. While dividend growth may indeed be tempered or curtailed for a bit, Turkcell has the borrowing capacity to not only fund its likely capex needs, but also maintain its dividend without destabilizing the balance sheet.

The Outlook

Modeling a company's financials through periods of high inflation is no simple task, and while inflation has eased some lately, a four-year low of 31% isn't exactly "back to normal". To that end, the outlook gets very hazy beyond the next couple of years. I do expect strong revenue growth over the next two years (in the mid-20%s), and I believe the launch of 5G services will have a significant positive impact on revenue and margins (as it has for many service providers). Longer term, I expect revenue growth to slow to a point where the long-term growth rate comes out in the mid-teens, but again, actual inflation will have a lot of impact here.

On the margin/cash flow side, I do expect Turkcell to maintain EBITDA margins in the low-to-mid-40%s for a few years, with more leverage coming down the line as 5G ramps and matures and as businesses like Paycell continue to grow. Long term, I still think mid-teens free cash flow margins are possible, driving over 20% annualized FCF growth (again, highly subject to inflation).

Discounting back, I get a fair value of around $7.50 for the ADRs today, with the main change from my prior target being more exact timing on the cash flow impacts of the 5G capex. Fundamentally, not much has really changed in my modeling assumptions. While I do like multiples-based valuation as a backup/backstop to discounted cash flows, it's tricky here. The market has been imposing a hefty discount on Turkish stocks; while a typical EBITDA multiple here would arguably be something in the 4x-5x range, the low end of that range drives a $13 fair value that just doesn't seem realistic now. Likewise with P/E, as unsustainable near-term EPS growth distorts that sort of approach.

The Bottom Line

With the share price and the business operating performance diverging some this year, Turkcell is more interesting to me than it was at the start of the year. I still have some concerns about management (including an arguably too-cozy relationship with the government), but I like the 5G strategy, and I'm still bullish on the potential for data- and payment-driven growth in the coming years, as well as the potential to continue reaping healthy margins. Today's price is tempting, but given my concerns about Türkiye's economy and corporate governance, I'm inclined to hold out for a bit better prospect return today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NIE: Reliable Dividends While Maintaining Exposure To Technology
data.description:
Overview

I believe that Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NIE) is a unique closed-end fund because it provides instant exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world while offering a high dividend yield. The underlying strategy may be a bit complex, but the fund has a long track record of success. When I previously covered NIE, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation. While I still believe that NIE is a great buy, I wanted to revisit the fund to specifically discuss some of the performance tradeoffs for the fund and how it's best utilized by investors.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NIE's share price has increased by about 3.8%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive momentum of the market over recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 12.9% over the same time frame. NIE now offers investors a starting yield of 7.8%, and the fund has a long history of maintaining consistent payouts.

Although the fund has seen its share price rise, NIE still trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. This means that it's still a solid time to accumulate shares for long-term income investors. The fund provides instant diversity across some of the highest quality businesses in the world, but investors should limit their expectations around its ability to provide attractive price growth over time. The dividends are likely to remain consistent for long-term investors, but there are some weaknesses that investors should consider. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy NIE implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, NIE has total net managed assets of $768.5M that is spread across 203 different holdings. Under normal market conditions, the fund will invest at least 80% of its assets into a combination of equities and convertible securities. These convertible securities operate as a hybrid instrument that generates income similar to a bond, but can be converted to common shares if management believes that a position has the potential for attractive growth in the future. The breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Equities: 56.92%
  • Convertible Securities: 37.57%
  • Cash: 5.51%

Although NIE doesn't specifically aim to mimic any specific industry, the fund's breakdown is a bit similar to the S&P 500. For instance, the fund is most heavily allocated to the Information Technology companies at 34.89% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Financials and Consumer Discretionary investments, which account for 11.36% and 11.27% of assets, respectively. When I previously covered NIE, the exposure to Information Technology only made up 25.82% of assets, so the fund has increased its allocation here quite a bit.

Since the fund is actively managed, there is a listed net expense ratio of 1.07%, which may be worth it considering the high yield and complexity of the fund's approach. Additionally, NIE implements some option writing against a portion of its assets to help amplify the income level that is generated within its holdings. However, the fund limits the option writing to 70% of the value of each stock position, but this can shift based on the market environment. This allows the fund to collect option premiums, which are then used to support the higher distributions.

Since the fund is most heavily allocated to technology companies, we can see that NIE provides exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. As we can see below, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest individual holding at 5.34% of assets. This is followed by exposure to other notable names such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Alphabet (GOOG), just to name a few.

Performance Insights & Weakness

Despite the fund providing exposure to some of the most rapidly growing companies in the world, the inclusion of an option strategy and the convertible structure limit the growth potential of the fund. To demonstrate this weakness, we can refer to the performance chart over the last ten years below. As we can see, NIE's share price has increased by about 36.9% over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 250% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

So I want to emphasize that a position in NIE only makes sense if you are an investor that is specifically seeking income generation. The fund's structure and prioritization of income have resulted in lower returns over time. If you are bullish on the outlook of the market and technology, you'd likely be better off with a more traditional index-tracking fund that can fully participate in the growth of the market. By choosing income generation today, you are actively choosing to have less money at the end of the road.

There is a lot of potential growth in the market over the next decade, and NIE may not be the best choice for investors looking to participate. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. Despite the fund providing exposure to companies that can directly capitalize on this growth, NIE's structure will ultimately limit returns.

This is why NIE consistently underperforms against more traditional index-tracking ETFs, such as the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). As we can see below, NIE's share price growth is minimal when compared to QQQ's growth of 444.7% and SPY's growth of 227.9%. So even though NIE provides a high dividend yield, this doesn't automatically translate to a high total return over time. However, this doesn't necessarily make NIE a bad fund. This just confirms that NIE is best utilized by income investors that may not be prioritizing total return potential.

Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, NIE traded at a discount to NAV of 8.4%. Due to the recent market pullback, NIE now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 10.07%. For reference, NIE has traded at an average discount to NAV of 10.03% over the last five years, so the slightly larger discount pricing can be an indication that it's a good time to accumulate shares. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that NIE's current price to NAV level is attractive relative to its historical range.

Since the time of my last coverage, NIE has released an updated semi-annual report for 2026. Net investment income has amounted to $0.08 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.22 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the year, NIE is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with its prior years. Unfortunately, net investment income levels are extremely weak, so this leaves the fund reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings.

Thankfully, technology stocks have continued to rise, and this has allowed NIE to capture net realized gains that can support the distributions paid. Additionally, the semi-annual report is only dated through the end of July, so the sector's growth in the following months isn't reflected here. The current portfolio structure and sector allocation has allowed NIE to provide an attractive level of NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $30.91 per share
  • 2022: $30.32 per share
  • 2023: $22.43 per share
  • 2024: $23.82 per share
  • 2025: $26.52 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $26.82 per share

Solid Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.50 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.8%. Looking at the payout history below, NIE has done an excellent job at maintaining consistent payouts over the last decade. The two spikes you can see in 2022 and 2023 were due to special distributions that paid out excess earnings. Since NIE has proven its ability to increase its NAV over time and produce earnings that exceed payouts, I believe that this makes NIE a great option for investors seeking a source of high-income generation.

The only drawback is that the distributions aren't paid out on a monthly basis. This may not be a big deal for some investors, but retirees that are seeking a supplemental source of income to fund lifestyle expenses may prefer these distributions hitting their account more frequently. If we take the fund's quarterly distribution and multiply it by four quarters, we get an estimated annual payout of $2.00 per share. The fund's latest reporting indicates that NIE has no problem generating earnings that can exceed this payout amount, which means that a dividend cut in the near-term is very unlikely.

Additionally, the fund aims to issue payouts in a tax-efficient manner if possible. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 71.2% of the distributions on a YTD basis were paid out using net realized long-term capital gains. Long-term capital gains are classified as qualified dividends, which have the most favorable tax treatment for investors. So even though the remaining portion of the distributions YTD were fueled by net realized short-term gains and net investment income, the majority focus on tax-efficient payouts limits the overall tax liability for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating at this time due to the attractive valuation and the potential to provide consistent income over time. The fund's strategy has proven to be efficient at participating in the growth of the market. Although the included option strategy and emphasis on income may limit long-term returns when compared to more traditional index-tracking ETFs, NIE can still be a great choice for investors that specifically want to prioritize a reliable stream of income while still getting equity exposure to some of the best growth companies in the world.

data.title: Waiting For A Sudden Surge: The Eurozone's Big 4
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski | Charlotte de Montpellier | Paolo Pizzoli | Ruben Dewitte

Germany: Waiting for the ketchup bottle effect

Germany has been both the eurozone’s greatest disappointment and its main source of hope this year. Mired in prolonged stagnation and burdened by many structural challenges, Germany remained the region’s growth laggard. At the same time, its historic U-turn on fiscal stimulus and the announcement of major infrastructure and defence investments raised expectations for a rebound, both for Germany and the eurozone as a whole. However, persistent political tensions and the absence of meaningful structural reforms have dampened these hopes. Even the planned public investments for 2025 have fallen significantly short of target. The sauce is stuck in the bottle.

So, the main question is whether Germany is fundamentally unable to make fiscal stimulus effective – or whether it is simply a matter of time. We believe it is the latter. Fiscal stimulus and investment often resemble a ketchup bottle: nothing comes out at first, and then suddenly everything pours out at once. While this won’t solve Germany’s structural problems, it should deliver a boost. We expect German GDP growth to approach 1% in 2026 and 2% in 2027.

France: Persistent fiscal deadlock and rising risk of early elections

A new budget is unlikely before year-end, as political manoeuvring ahead of the 2027 presidential race is blocking consensus between the Senate and National Assembly. The most probable outcome is a rollover of the 2025 budget, with debates restarting almost from scratch in January. An agreement will remain elusive, raising the risk of government collapse and early legislative elections next year.

Although the right-wing RN leads polls at around 35%, it is unlikely to win a majority, meaning new elections would do little to resolve the deadlock. Without a budget, fiscal efforts will falter, and public finances will deteriorate, with the deficit widening to about 5.7% of GDP if the situation persists throughout 2026, up from 5.4% in 2025, well above the 4.6% target promised by the Lecornu government. While the absence of fiscal tightening may offer some support, higher financing costs and persistent uncertainty will offset this, leaving growth at just 0.8% versus 1.1% for the eurozone.

Italy: Still a domestic demand story

The effect of higher US tariffs on Italian external accounts will likely play out more clearly over 2026, when we expect net exports to act as a drag on economic growth. This will shift the burden of growth to domestic demand. Gross fixed capital formation should remain the primary driver, supported by infrastructure investment foreseen under the EU-funded National Recovery and Resilience Plan, which is due to formally expire over the summer of 2026.

Non-restrictive European Central Bank monetary policy could also help revive machinery investment. Private consumption, which has been disappointingly weak over 2025, might catch up a little next year. A resilient labour market, the ongoing recovery in purchasing power driven by decent wage dynamics and low inflation, and an extension of personal income tax reductions introduced by the budget might encourage households to reduce their savings ratios from current high levels.

Fiscal policy, however, will provide little additional support. The draft budget under discussion in parliament is neutral by design, reflecting the government’s clear priority of maintaining the adjustment path and ensuring Italy exits the Excessive Deficit Procedure in early 2026.

Spain: Looking for a quality turning point

Spain’s economy remains a eurozone outperformer, with 2025 growth nearing 3%. However, much of this momentum is quantity-driven: immigration has increased the population, making Spain’s performance less impressive when measured by GDP per capita or labour productivity gains. The key challenge now is to make growth more quality-led, driven by higher productivity. This will be especially crucial in 2026, as the contribution of government spending growth will be limited in the absence of a budget, net exports will be muted partly due to the strong euro, and private consumption growth is normalising.

Investment is set to play a pivotal role, with the EU’s Recovery and Resilience Facility (RRF) providing significant upside: only about €59 billion of the possible €163 billion has been awarded so far. While the productivity impact may not be immediate, these investments could mark a turning point towards quality-driven growth and help keep Spain ahead of its eurozone peers in 2026 and beyond.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Kinder Morgan: I Would've Entered If Not For Liquidity And Valuation Risks
data.description:
The energy market has faced volatility for the whole year amid stubborn inflation, tariff woes, and geopolitical tensions. Yet, natural gas shows newer and bigger growth prospects amid the evolving market landscape. Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) may benefit from it due to its heavy involvement in natural gas pipelines. However, its liquidity is concerning at this point and may hinder its potential expansion. Valuation also seems to adhere to them due to some downside risks, while technicals suggest early bearish tendencies.

KMI Q3 2025: A Brief Recap Of Its Decent Performance

Natural gas and oil prices weakened during the third quarter of 2025. We can attribute it to seasonality, several interlocking supply-demand dynamics, and market structure causes. Stronger natural gas production led to unused supply and gas inventories piling up. This reduced natural gas scarcity and pushed the price down. Milder weather conditions were also a factor. Meanwhile, increased global LNG supply reduced the need to buy at higher spot rates.

Kinder Morgan, Inc. has taken advantage of the recent natural gas trends. After all, short-term trends do not necessarily tarnish long-term views. Its Natural Gas Pipelines or NGP segment also continued to receive agreements and projects. One example was its binding firm transportation agreements with an anchor shipper to support its $93M South Texas Enhancement Projects or STEP. KMI also offset the impact of lower prices by raising its production volume in the NGP segment. Meanwhile, KMI reduced the volume in its Product Pipelines segment in line with pricing differences and rising cost pressures. As a result, its revenues in Q3 2025 amounted to $4.15B, up by 11.2% YoY from $3.70B.

The challenge is that inflationary headwinds have already caught up and impacted its costs and expenses. For instance, its cost of sales rose 36.2% YoY and offset revenue growth. Its percentage to the total operating costs and expenses increased from 38.2% to 45.2%. This means that KMI became more cost-sensitive during the quarter. The consolation was that revenues were still high enough to cover all this. Hence, it stayed profitable even if the operating margin decreased to 25.6% versus 27.4% YoY.

Drivers

Natural gas may remain a primary source of KMI’s success. This may be driven by the global data center expansion. In 2025-2030, the global data center capacity demand may increase by 3.5x. This may lead to a substantial increase in electricity demand. By 2030, US data center electricity may reach 606 TWh. As a main electricity source, natural gas may benefit from these trends. I think the market has already started to price in this aspect as the natural gas price skyrockets. It is already very close to $5.00 after its sharp upsurge last month. This may sustain its revenue increase this quarter.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation is a big challenge for KMI despite the solid growth prospects for natural gas. At 3.0%, it remains at its 14-month high. This can raise cost pressures across industries, and KMI is no exception. It may also have to raise wages, so employees can keep up with the rising prices. This can also lead to weaker purchasing power and pricing flexibility, which can squeeze margins more. New tariffs must also be considered due to their inflationary nature.

Increasing campaigns against CO2 emissions may also become a risk factor for KMI. Although its CO2 segment does not refer to emissions, it owns natural underground CO2 reservoirs in the southwestern part of the US. It also has CO2 pipelines and projects related to CO2 transportation for CCS or carbon capture and sequestration. Lastly, it uses CO2 to extract extra oil from mature fields like the Permian Basin. In line with these campaigns, the calls for renewable energy to replace other energy sources and stop CO2 usage and emissions have increased in recent years. For instance, renewables comprised 23% of electricity sources in the US. This is projected to rise to 25% this year and 27% in 2026.

Meanwhile, lower oil prices can be a double-edged sword for the company. Now, Brent and WTI are close to their 12-month low. This can impact the selling price of its produced oil.

Lastly, I am concerned about its liquidity. It remained profitable despite higher cost pressures. So, it generated enough operating cash flow to cover capex. However, the FCF of $621M was not enough to cover dividends of $654M. It was also low for its borrowing repayments. So, it had to issue $1.91B in borrowings. This may suggest that its profits are not enough to sustain its operating capacity. Otherwise, it wouldn’t have relied heavily on borrowings.

As you can see, its debt level remains high at over $30B versus the current cash and cash equivalents of only $76M. Also, its Net Debt/EBITDA is quite high at 4.8x. This means that KMI needs almost five years to repay the whole amount in a single payment. The consolation is that KMI may always borrow or refinance to ensure liquidity. But considering its capital intensity, it may have a hard time financing its expansion in line with the natural gas growth prospects. It may have to borrow again to do so. After all, it does not have enough cash to bleed out.

Valuation

Price ratios highlight KMI’s overvaluation. For instance, P/B shows that the stock trades at 1.94x its book value per share versus the five-year average of only 1.29x. This is also nearly double the ideal ratio of 1.00x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price will be $17.81. The same applies to P/S, which suggests that KMI has already increased more than necessary relative to its sales or revenue. It trades above the historical average and the TP of $19.17. Meanwhile, P/E shows that the stock is cheap relative to the EPS considering the TP of $92.48. However, we should note the outlier in 2020, which can lead to unnecessary biases. Without it, we can see that the stock is trading at its highest level since 2021. If we concentrate on the past four years, the TP will be $17.92 (($26.80/(21.97x/17.93x)).

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. For simplicity, I used the annualized dividends of $1.17 based on the quarterly payout of $0.2925. This is also reported on Seeking Alpha. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 5.5%. This is slightly higher than the standard of 5.0% to be conservative. But this is lower than my typical risk premium of 6.0% since I believe natural gas has a lot of growth drivers to offset market risks. Lastly, I estimated Beta using the SLOPE function on MS Excel. I believe this is precise since it is close to the reported value of 0.67 on Yahoo Finance. Using all this information, I derived a TP of $21.49. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $20.41. This proves that KMI is already expensive.

Technicals

KMI’s technicals warrant caution amid early bearish signals. The 50-day SMA has already crossed below the 200-day SMA. This suggests that the recent average price trend is lower than the average in the past 200 days. We must also note the selloff last October 23. The price is now consolidating at the same level right before it took place. In addition, the price is already trending below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing weaker momentum.

Its MACD and RSI can confirm our observations. The MACD line is still above the signal line, which shows that buying volume has increased recently. However, the histogram is decreasing and approaching the neutral zone. This implies that selling volume is also increasing and may outpace buying volume. Meanwhile, RSI presents consolidation, showing that the price rebound was mainly due to the recent overselling. This also shows a flat trend and that entering at this level can have some downside risks.

Conclusion

Kinder Morgan, Inc. remains sound, driven by natural gas demand and potential growth prospects. However, inflationary headwinds and liquidity risks must be considered to assess if KMI can take advantage of natural gas expansion in the long run. Its valuation is also high, so entering at this price does not seem enticing. Technicals adhere to it as bearish signals emerge. I advise investors to stay on the sidelines and give a hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Recovery, Inflation And Burden Sharing: 3 Calls For The Eurozone
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski | Peter Vanden Houte | Bert Colijn

ING's base call: Manufacturing will see a cyclical recovery

It has to happen at some point - a turnaround in the eurozone manufacturing sector, and we think we'll see it next year. It's been struggling since 2022 on the back of high energy prices, a strengthening euro, intensifying Chinese competition and the trade war. While headwinds from the latter have not disappeared, and some, of course, are structural, both oil and natural gas prices have declined by more than 20% since the beginning of 2025. In addition, Germany intends to significantly lower electricity costs for energy-intensive industries, providing additional breathing space.

On the demand side, we must not forget that the remaining money from the EU’s recovery fund must be spent in 2026, while German infrastructure works and increased military spending will also start to have an impact. Capacity utilisation in manufacturing has already risen throughout the year, which might open the window for some pick-up in business investment next year. All in all, it looks as if manufacturing will see growth in 2026, even if the structural headaches, particularly Chinese competition, seem here to stay.

Our risky call: Eurozone inflation could undershoot significantly in 2026

Even though the economy seems set for a cyclical improvement, inflation could still undershoot more markedly this year, not because of weak domestic demand but rather because of external factors.

Energy prices, for example, could very well come in lower than expected due to sluggish global demand and increased expectations of supply, while the stronger euro is set to weigh on import prices again next year. Furthermore, with growing Chinese competition and European producers redirecting goods that would have been exported to the US back into the European market, the risk of price dumping is real. Consequently, import prices for key goods could face continued pressure.

So, while domestic demand could lead to some improvement in economic growth, there is a clear possibility that inflation undershoots. Sub-1.5% is not unimaginable. Many of these factors are temporary, and medium-term upside risks to inflation remain, but the European Central Bank could feel pressure to lower rates so as not to get caught in a reverse ‘team transitory’ scenario.

Our bold call: Eurobonds are coming but in a different wrapping

More than a year after the release of the Draghi report, tangible progress in Europe – whether on deeper integration, reducing regulation, tackling market and financial fragmentation, or reforming energy and telecommunications - remains elusive. Expecting the full implementation of all of Draghi's recommendations in 2026 would be far too bold a call. Instead, the greater risk is that national governments prioritise their own economies, relegating the European dimension to a secondary concern at best.

Yet, despite persistent resistance, particularly among core eurozone countries, towards burden-sharing at the European level, 2026 could still deliver a surprise. Given the EU's positive experience with project bonds and the Recovery Fund, a 'Ukraine bond' to finance additional military aid or reconstruction could gain broad support. While Germany’s substantial fiscal stimulus has, to some extent, reduced the urgency for new safe assets, such a bond would represent another step towards completing the capital markets union and revive a familiar European tradition: introducing Eurobonds through the back door.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Hess Midstream: A Compelling Idea For 2026
data.description:
Hess Midstream (HESM) has been a poor performer over the past year, losing 8% of its value with particular weakness over the past three months. Concerns about slower long-term growth and Chevron’s (CVX) Bakken drilling plans have greatly dampened enthusiasm around HESM. I last covered Hess Midstream in September, reiterating the stock as a “strong buy” given my view investors had become too concerned about production growth in the Bakken. Since then, shares have lost about 2%, suggesting this call has been, at the least, early. With updated financials and 2026 coming into clearer view, now is a good time to revisit HESM. I view the pessimism as greatly overstated.

The weakness really began with CVX moving to a three-rig program in the Bakken from a four-rig program, reducing long-term production growth potential. As a result, HESM is pivoting away from focusing on growth, given more muted drilling by Chevron and is focusing on maximizing free cash flow for shareholder returns. Chevron is focused on sustaining oil production at about 200,000 barrels a day. I would note this fully protect HESM’s existing cash flow. This week, Chevron announced plans to spend $18 billion on cap-ex in 2026, at the low end of its $18-21 billion multiyear target, speaking to its focus on capital efficiency in this commodity price environment. About one-third of this budget will be spent on US shale plays, including the Bakken, which is most relevant to HESM’s results.

Now, I would emphasize that these fears are more about the future than the present. In the company’s third quarter reported on November 3rd, Hess Midstream earned $0.75, which beat estimates by $0.06. Revenue grew by 11% to $421 million, a reminder that while investors have become fearful about the future, existing results are still very strong.

The company now expects 2025 EBITDA of $1.245-1.255 billion within its prior $1.235-$1.285 billion range. At the midpoint, EBITDA is up 10% from last year. It cut its cap-ex outlook by another $30 million as it has suspended construction at the Capa gas plant and is walking away from the project. Free cash flow should be about $765 million. Sustaining cap-ex is about $125 million. Now some growth projects are underway, so in practice, I view minimum cap-ex as closer to $175-200 million, but this still provides room to reduce cap-ex further and improve free cash flow next year.

In Q3, HESM generated $321 million of EBITDA up from $287 million last year thanks to revenue growth and an expansion in margins to 82% from 81%. Adjusted free cash flow was $187 million up from $141 million last year given higher EBITDA and lower cap-ex as HESM ramps down growth spending. As you can see below, volumes across Hess’s system are still growing. Natural gas activity leads the way with processing growing over 10%.

While growth will slow, it is important to remember that HESM has a strong contract with Chevron (which inherited Hess’s role in the contract) that provides stable cash flow through 2033. Minimum volume commitments are set on a 3-year forward basis, and once set, they can never go down. They are set at 80% of anticipated production levels. Additionally, HESM’s fees rise over time, generally rising at CPI through the life of the contract. This provides structural revenue growth and insulates margins from potential inflation pressures.

Now, volumes across HESM’s system are running above MVC levels. While MVCs protect the vast majority of revenue, revenue could in theory fall from current levels if CVX production fell substantially. While I like to see a pipeline company have substantial MVC protection, I also do prefer to see volumes run above MVC levels. If volumes are below MVC levels, that points to long-term challenges with difficulty re-contracting when the contract matures whereas robust volumes speak to a durable model. In that way, MVC payments are more akin to a one-time windfall, as all contracts come to an end eventually, though HESM does have 8 more years on its contract with CVX, providing meaningful visibility.

Now, the stock currently has an 8.8% distribution yield, and management targets ongoing distribution growth of 5% through 2027. As reiterated on the earnings call, management can grow its distribution by 5% per year and cover it with cash flow, just from the MVCs. With volumes above their minimums this year, HESM should generate over $150 million of free cash flow after distributions this year. I expect about $50 million of free cash flow growth next year. That should enable about $200 million of buybacks beyond its distribution. That would reduce its unit count by 2-3%, essentially enabling half of its ~5% distribution/unit target without actually increasing the total cash cost of distributions.

During the quarter, the company bought back $100 million of stock. With where the stock is trading, I view buybacks as very accretive. With its equity yielding over 8%, one could even argue for debt-funded buybacks as the saved distribution costs would more than cover incremental cash interest expense. While I think debt financed buybacks could be justified, given the company’s conservative financial policy, I do not view them as likely. Buybacks will likely just be funded out of cash flow, and I do not expect the firm to consider increasing financial leverage. Leverage currently sits at 3x, which is consistent with HESM’s target.

I expect Chevron to proportionally participate in all future buybacks in order to maintain its ownership stake at the current 38% level. I saw Chevron’s investor day as a potential time for management to discuss its future plans for HESM. Instead, HESM received only passing mention with CVX simply acknowledging its 38% stake, and Chevron is not showing any signs of its next steps. I do not see a scenario where it would reduce its ownership stake as likely.

HESM should exit 2027 with an annualized distribution of about $3.33, giving the stock a 9.8% yield at the current price. Essentially, we do not need to see any cash flow growth beyond this point to provide investors a ~10% long-term return. In fact with the CPI riders, we could likely see 1-2% annual production declines beyond 2027 and still support the distribution.

This is why I view HESM as a compelling value opportunity. MVCs significantly truncate potential downside, and it does not even need any growth to provide a market-beating long-term return. With natural gas volumes likely to continue growing (especially as increasing electricity usage from data centers and export capacity support natural gas prices). This is also why longer term I view Chevron as likely to seek to acquire the 62% of HESM it does not own.

With a cost of debt closer to ~5%, this would be an immediately accretive acquisition while also simplifying its capital structure. I expect Chevron to be patient, and the longer the stock remains around $35, the more it may see a chance to successfully acquire HESM at closer to $40 than the $45 I once thought it would take to get majority support for a transaction. While timing is unknowable, I view CVX acquiring HESM as a distinct possibility in 2026. To be clear, I view HESM’s return prospects as attractive as a standalone entity with M&A premium just a sweetener to the bull case.

As such, I remain a “strong buy” on HESM and view it as an attractive long-term investment for income-oriented investors. Investors collect a nearly-9% yield, more than fully covered by free cash flow. Plus, ongoing distribution growth is fully covered by its MVCs. Its balance sheet is solid, and a multiyear return of 12-13% is my base case as I expect distribution growth to persist beyond 2027. A potential takeout by CVX could also accelerate returns. As investors grow more comfortable with underlying cash flow, even absent growth, I expect HESM to gradually move higher and towards $40. I rate it a “strong buy” and view HESM as one of the top values in the market.

data.title: Fund Your Retirement With High Yield Bonds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

High Yield Bonds offer higher returns with a little more risk than investment-grade bonds. It is critical, however, to examine the bond's default risk - credit ratings, ability to pay the interest due, and maturity length. This article summarizes my approach to select high yield bonds with superior returns and with acceptable risks. Bonds are assessed from a default risk perspective, and those with unacceptable risk of default are eliminated from consideration. The result is a recommended high yield bond portfolio with yield to maturities ranging from 5% to 34% with an average yield to maturity of 9%.

Background

I am a retired engineer. I have a diversified portfolio composed of equity funds, individual stocks, bond funds, and individual bonds. I invest in bonds and dividend stocks to provide the cash flow required to fund my retirement. I perform a quantitative analysis to select individual stocks and bonds.

Approximately 25% of my portfolio is in fixed income. Of the fixed income, 78% is in individual bonds, 16% in bond funds, and 6% in money market funds.

I recently published articles summarizing my individual stocks and equity and fixed income funds.

The article below discusses my individual bonds - selection criteria and recommended bonds.

High Yield Bonds Risk

High yield bonds offer higher returns with a little more risk than investment-grade bonds. The principal risk to high yield bonds is that the company issuing the bond defaults on them… declaring bankruptcy. Bonds do not go to zero value if the company declares bankruptcy. Bonds are senior in the credit order and must be made well before secured debt, preferred stock, or equity stock.

I have a personal example of the impact of company default on bond returns. About 5 years ago I bought a B rated bond that declared Chapter 11 a year after I bought it. The bond still traded on the market while the company was in chapter 11. I sold the bond for more than I paid for it. Including interest, my total gain was 34% on the bond. The second bond was a B- rated bond. After I bought the bond, its price deteriorated. I doubled down and bought more of the bonds. The company declared Chapter 11 2/3rds of a year after I bought the first bond. Its bonds still traded, however, on the stock market, and its price improved during the Chapter 11 reorganization process. I sold the bonds, and my total gains were 6% for the first bonds and 73% for the second. The last bond was a CCC+ rated bond that declared bankruptcy. I lost 78% on that bond. Overall, my average gain for all of the defaulted bonds was 34%. So default is not the end of the world for an individual bond holding. That said, I now do a more extensive assessment of bond default risk and have not had any bonds default using the selection methods I will summarize below.

Bond probability of default is a function of credit risk, ability to pay the interest, and time before the bond matures. Overall bond default rates as a function of credit rating and time are shown below. As you can see, longer maturity bonds have a higher risk of default than shorter maturity bonds.

Below is a plot of default probability versus credit ratings and time. Reduce default risk by picking high-yield bonds with better credit ratings and shorter time horizons.

One of my criteria for high yield bond selection is a maturity of 5 years or less. I want bonds that I can conceivably hold to maturity, which makes the total return independent of interest rate changes. And 5 years of less is a good maturity for minimizing default risk. BB credit rating is a good lower credit limit for purchasing high yield bonds. This article comes to the conclusion that BB default rates are negligibly higher than BBBs and significantly outperform BBBs.

High-Yield Bond Returns

High-yield bonds offer higher returns even with the greater risk of default than investment-grade bonds. This article says the return of bonds correlates to its initial yield to worse.

This chart from the article looks at bond returns over the last 35 years. Higher yield-to-worse bonds gave higher returns than lower yield-to-worse bonds even with their higher default risk.

The better long-term returns of high yield bonds versus investment grade bonds can be seen comparing two Vanguard bond funds.

The Vanguard High Yield fund has a higher yield and better long-term returns than their investment grade intermediate bond fund. The high yield fund is only a little more volatile than the investment-grade fund. In addition, the high yield fund dropped less during the COVID recession than the investment grade bond fund. The Vanguard High Yield fund contains about 1000 bonds. An individual investor can cherry pick a few high yield bonds that offer better returns than the Vanguard bond conglomerate will.

Bond Selection Criteria

I use the following criteria to select high yield bonds

  • Maturity 5 years of less
  • Credit ratings better than CCC
  • Close examination of the company Debt to EBITDA. Debt to EBITDA is a good indicator of the ability to pay interest. Debt/EBITDA 5 or less is ideal. A+ rated Toyota Debt/EBITDA is just under 5 (4.6). 10 is acceptable. Above ten acceptable if the yield to maturity is much higher than the YTM versus Debt/EBITDA trendline
  • Select bonds that have above average YTM versus credit risk than the trendline
  • Select bonds that have above average YTM versus Debt/EBITDA trendline

Bond Yield to Maturity versus Risk

I've examined a number of bonds. These include senior bonds available for sale on the Vanguard website as well as baby bonds available for sale on the stock exchange. Baby bonds have a credit priority just under senior bonds and secured debt. Baby bonds rank higher than preferred or equity stock. I've chosen the highest yielding bonds versus credit risk. The table below summarizes the best high yield bonds I've analyzed. The table lists the company, bond symbol, bond type, CUSPID for senior bonds, bond credit rating, coupon, maturity date, Debt to EBITDA, current yield, Yield to maturity, and rate of default for bonds that have a credit rating. Credit ratings come from S&P Global Ratings, Moody's, and Egan-Jones rating of baby bonds when initially issued.

A plot of Yield to maturity versus credit rating is shown below.

A plot of Yield to maturity versus credit rating is shown below.

I currently own the bonds labeled in blue. In addition, I have a large position (23% of individual bond portfolio) in Treasury Bonds. I may adjust my bond portfolio based on this analysis.

Recommended High Yield Bonds

The bonds below are my current recommended individual high yield bond portfolio. I eliminated bonds that either had too high a credit or Debt/EBITDA risk. I selected the bonds which have higher yield to maturity than the trendlines. The average maturity of the bond portfolio is 2.48 years. Debt/EBITDA is an acceptable 5.96. Current yield is 6.63% with a yield to maturity of 9%.

A plot of Yield to maturity versus credit rating for recommended bonds labeled in blue is shown below.

A plot of Yield to Maturity versus Debt/EBITDA for recommended bonds labeled in blue is shown below.

These bonds would be a good starting point to build a high yield bond portfolio with acceptable risk.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tracking Bill Ackman's Pershing Square 13F Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Pershing Square's 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Ackman's regulatory 13F form filed on 11/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings article for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q2 2025.

Ackman's 13F portfolio value increased from ~$13.73B to ~$14.64B this quarter. The number of positions remained steady at 10. The portfolio remains heavily concentrated with a few huge bets. The top three positions account for ~59% of the total portfolio value: Uber Technologies, Brookfield Corp, and Alphabet.

Their flagship fund's (2004 inception) track record is excellent, with annualized returns of ~16% compared to ~10% for the S&P 500 index. YTD as of late November, they are up ~23%. In addition to partner stakes, the fund also invests the capital from Pershing Square Holdings (OTCPK:PSHZF), a public entity that debuted in Euronext Amsterdam in October 2014. This was set up primarily to increase the amount of capital invested that is permanent. It has always traded at a discount to NAV. It currently trades at $66.55 per share compared to an NAV of ~$87.

Note 1: Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) are other long positions in the partnership - the holdings were disclosed in 13D filings on November 15, 2013 - as they are not 13F securities, they are not listed in the 13F report. Ackman held just under 10% of the outstanding shares of both these businesses - 115.57M shares of FNMA at a cost basis of $2.29 and 63.5M shares of FMCC at a cost-basis of $2.14. The combined investment outlay was ~$400M. FNMA currently trades at $11.49 & FMCC at $10.85 per share. In March 2018, Pershing Square said their Fannie/Freddie pfds now amount to 21% of the total investment in the two GSEs. Their Q1 2025 investor call mentioned the following: Fannie/Freddie common shares could become an anchor for the sovereign wealth fund as they are uniquely situated as forever assets that can grow, given growth is the only way to solve the country's deficit problem. The low-30s price estimate provided in our January presentation upon successful exit from conservatorship may turn out to be low, given Pulte's operational, management, and board changes, which are solid steps toward running them as real private businesses rather than government mandated.

Note 2: Pershing Square has a ~6.2% ownership stake in UMG Music Group (OTCPK:UMGNF) at a cost-basis of ~€18 per share. It is down from ~10% as of Q4 2024, It currently trades at ~€21.48. It is still their largest equity stake. The position is not in their 13F report, as it is not US listed. Pershing Square said the following regarding the reduction in UMG: "UMG has substantially outperformed the rest of our portfolio (other than the GSEs) year to date in light of its extremely strong recent results. UMG was our largest position going into the day at more than ~27% of our capital. After the sale, it will still be our largest position at about ~17% of the portfolio."

Note 3: Pershing Square Tontine Holdings (PSTH), the liquidated SPAC, distributed SPARC shares (not tradeable currently) to previous PSTH common and warrant holders following its successful registration during Q3 2023.

Note 4: Pershing Square had a significant ownership stake in Nike (NKE) through deep-in-the-money calls. The position was fully disposed after the quarter ended.

To learn more about Bill Ackman, check out the book "Confidence Game: How Hedge Fund Manager Bill Ackman Called Wall Street's Bluff".

Stake Decreases

Uber Technologies (UBER): UBER is currently the top position in the 13F portfolio at 20.25%. It was purchased during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$61 and ~$82. The stock currently trades at $90.99. This quarter saw marginal trimming.

Note: Their cost-basis is in the high-60s. The investment thesis is based on the idea that ride hailing won't be a winner-takes-all business. In that context, Uber will have a central role to play.

Brookfield (BN): BN is now a large (top three) position at 19.21% of the portfolio. The stake was established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$25 and ~$30. The bulk of the position was built during the next quarter at prices between ~$27 and ~$36. There was a ~18% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$33 and ~$42 The stock currently trades at $46.72. There was a marginal trimming this quarter.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 3-for-2 stock-split in October.

Alphabet (GOOGL): GOOGL is now the third largest 13F stake at ~19% of the portfolio. It was purchased during March 2023 in the low-90s price range. There was a ~13% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$104 and ~$128. That was followed by a ~20% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$117 and ~$139. Q2 2024 saw a ~16% trimming at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. The stock currently trades at ~$318. There was a ~4% trimming this quarter.

Restaurant Brands International (QSR): The QSR stake is a large ~10% of the portfolio stake. Pershing Square's original cost-basis was ~$16. Q3 2017 saw a ~32% selling at prices between $59 and $66. That was followed with a ~22% reduction in H1 2018 at prices between $53 and $64. The four quarters through Q3 2019 also saw a ~28% selling at prices between $52 and $79. In June 2020, they increased the position by roughly two-thirds through forward contracts at $44.20 cost-basis. The stock currently trades at $71.93. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Note: They own ~7.4% of the business.

Kept Steady:

Howard Hughes (HHH): HHH is now a 10.58% of the 13F portfolio position. The stake was first established in 2010 as a result of its spin-off from GGP Inc. The majority of the current stake is from the addition of ~10M shares in Q1 2020 at ~$50 per share through a private placement. Q1 2021 saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$78 and ~$102. Q4 2022 saw another ~17% stake increase at an average cost of ~$72 per share. Q4 2023 saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between ~$66 and ~$87. The stock is currently at $87.18.

Note: Regulatory filings show them owning 27.85M shares (46.9% of business) - in August, they acquired 9M shares of HHH at $100 per share and plans to transform the company into a diversified holding company with an insurance operation in the style of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

Amazon.com (AMZN): AMZN is a large 8.73% of the portfolio stake established during the last quarter at prices between ~$161 and ~$224. The stock currently trades at ~$229.

Note: Their overall cost-basis is $175 per share.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG): CMG is now a 5.77% of the portfolio position. The stake was established in Q3 2016 at a cost-basis of ~$8.10 per share. The position was sold down by ~30% in Q3 2018 in the high-single-digit price-range and that was followed with a ~17% trimming over the next four quarters. Q1 2020 also saw a one-third selling at ~$17.20 per share. There was a ~7% trimming in Q1 2021 at ~$26.80 per share. Q3 2021 saw a ~3% increase at ~$38.20 per share. There was a ~14% trimming over the two quarters through Q2 2023 at prices between ~$27.30 and ~$42.80. Q4 2023 saw a ~14% trimming at prices between ~$35.60 and ~$46.80. The next quarter also saw a ~10% trimming at prices between ~$44.30 and ~$59.10. Q2 2024 saw another ~22% reduction at prices between ~$57 and ~$69. That was followed by a ~14% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between $55.73 and $66.16. The next quarter saw a ~13% further reduction at prices between $47.55 and $61.16. The stock currently trades at $34.15.

Note: Pershing Square has fully exited this position after the quarter ended.

Hilton Worldwide Holdings (HLT): The 5.37% portfolio stake was established in October 2018. It was purchased at prices between $64 and $78. Q1 2020 saw a ~30% stake increase at a cost-basis of ~$70. Q1 2022 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$129 and ~$158. There was a ~7% trimming during Q1 2023 while Q3 2023 saw a ~10% increase. The next quarter saw a ~11% selling at prices between ~$147 and ~$182. There was a ~18% trimming during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$202 and ~$234. That was followed by a ~26% reduction during the next quarter at prices between $229.56 and $258.32. There was a ~45% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$219 and ~$275. The stock currently trades at ~$274.

Note 1: Their overall cost-basis is ~$72 per share.

Note 2: In Q4 2018, Hilton Worldwide Holdings came back into the portfolio after a gap of eighteen months. The previous position was purchased in Q3 2016 and disposed of a year later. Pershing Square has said that the new position was acquired at a better valuation compared to their previous purchase. Also, the business structure has transformed into a capital-light model following the spinoff in early 2017 of Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) and Hilton Grand Vacations (HGV).

Hertz Global (HTZ): The 0.71% HTZ stake was established during Q1 2025 at prices between $3.26 and $4.74. The stock currently trades at $5.14.

Note: Their cost basis is ~$3.80, and their ownership stake is ~20%. They highlighted its asymmetric range of outcomes and several-fold return potential during their Q1 2025 investor call.

Seaport Entertainment (SEG): SEG was spun off from Howard Hughes in early August 2024. Shareholders received one share for every nine shares of HHH held. It started trading at ~$22 and currently goes for $21.38. There was a ~20% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between $26.12 and $33.86.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Pershing Square's 13F stock holdings in Q3 2025:

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adtalem Global: Solid Healthcare Education Platform, But Investigation Risk Limits Upside
data.description:
I have decided to follow-up Adtalem Global Education (ATGE). At the time of my last article, I rated the stock as a buy due to it's strong student base and clear growth. I am circling back to this company, because I have noticed quite a drop past month. Today the company is not a typical higher education business. This company transformed into a U.S. healthcare system component. The main focus is to supply the sector with constant flow of medical professionals. The sector is suffering from the deficit, which is quite deep and systemic. U.S. healthcare is experiencing the highest shortage in history. Yet this issue does not apply to the U.S. alone, my wife is a nurse, and trust me, my country has the same issue, if not bigger. The shortage in US is expected to reach more than 1.2 million of new specialists. In my opinion, this gap between supply and demand is not cyclical or temporary. This is a long-term structural issue, which is following demographic tendencies. In other words, the number of people that need healthcare services increases, while doctors and nurses suffer from professional burnout, also lets not forget to add high barrier of entry into studies and retirements each year.

Business Overview

In my opinion, ATGE is one of the biggest healthcare education services providers in US. They are positioning themselves as systematically important component in U.S. healthcare system and that is true in one way or another. Their business model is simple: provide career-oriented education. That means they are generating revenue from students and study programs.

Chamberlain University is where they teach nursing and healthcare management programs. In my opinion, it's going to be the main driver for a long time, unless recent shareholder inquiries reveal issues.

Walden University offers more programs, including healthcare, business, IT and psychology. They acquired this asset in 2020-2021 and it strengthened Adtalem's position in digital education, which was a very smart move.

Medicine and Veterinary schools consist of American University of the Caribbean School of Medicine, Ross University School of Medicine also Ross University School of Veterinary Medicine. These institutions are preparing doctors and veterinarians, which are high demand specialties.

Also, as I mentioned, ATGE is using hybrid learning. This lets them reach more students and give flexibility. Adding technology integration like AI programs, are strengthening their position too. Partnership with Google Cloud to create AI qualification program for healthcare students and specialists is a logical step. They understand that technology is not going anywhere. I believe in a long-run it's going to give them competitive advantage.

Financial Health and Growth

Let's look at the numbers. Revenue in Q1 FY2026 was $462.3 million, which is 10.8% more than a year ago. That is a good revenue growth, given that analysts were expecting lower (around $452.3 million). Revenue growth shows that demand for their programs remains strong. EPS was $1.67, compared to $1.18 a year ago. Adjusted EPS reached $1.75, and that is more than 35.7% growth compared to $1.29 a year ago. To me, it is very impressive result and it shows that they are managing costs and effectiveness.

Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 24.2%, compared to 23.2% a year ago and also shows that they are not only growing the revenue but increasing profitability.

Enrollment reached 97359, around 8% more than a year ago. And it marked ninth month when student numbers are growing, which in my opinion is very important in education business.

Chamberlain University enrollments grew 2.2% per year. Even though the growth might seem slower, it's still growth.

Walden University enrollments grew by 13.6% per year reaching the highest level in the university's history. And it's clear that Walden is successful, maybe because of their wider range of programs and digital education model.

Medicine and Veterinary segment grew by 2.4% per year, also showing stable growth, even though it is not that fast.

For FY2026 the company is expecting that annual revenue will reach $1.90 billion and that would mean 6% growth. Adjusted EPS is expected at $7.6-7.9 range, which would be 14-18.5% annual growth. I believe that these plans are quite ambitious and very possible given the current results we see.

Risks

In late October 2025 it was announced that DJS Law group initiated investor inquiry into Adtalem's possible violations of the securities laws. The investigation, according to the law firm, "focuses on whether the Company issued misleading statements and/or failed to disclose information pertinent to investors." Keep in mind this is not a formal regulatory investigation and does not imply that they are guilty, quite a normal process for lawyers to gather information on behalf of shareholders. Nevertheless share price fell almost 30%, which shows that the market is very sensitive to news like that. Such a situation, in my eyes, increases uncertainty and therefore I am more cautious as well. I will try to cover the stock whenever more information arises.

Let's say investigation finds that Chamberlain is really slowing, this would pose a new risk. The problems will expand into other segments and we might see corrected growth numbers, which will also lead to a sharp decline. Keep in mind that retail investors usually sell first, and only then read what happened.

Another risk, maybe not major like the main one is that a lot of students are depending on federal financial aid (Title IV). This means that Adtalem is sensitive to any federal financing changes, and securities fraud investigation is not going to help them in this case.

Summary

Even though Adtalem Global Education is showing strong financial results and operates in good market, the risk of securities fraud allegation is forcing me to be cautious until we see things clear. I believe that any investor should be aware of the situation, it is not about the growth currently buy about being clear on the situation, I will try to keep you posted regarding it. I will be re-rating this stock as hold until allegations clear up.

data.title: Beware Of The Unwinding Japanese Carry Trade
data.description:
On Wall Street, it is said of the carry trade that first they carry you in, and then they carry you out. By this, it is meant that a lot of money can be made by borrowing in a depreciating currency that has low interest rates and lending in an appreciating currency that has high interest rates.

The catch, however, was that you could lose your shirt if the currency in which you borrowed began to yield higher interest rates and started to appreciate. This would be especially the case at a time when the interest rates of the currency in which you lent started to decline, and that currency started to depreciate.

All of this would seem to be of utmost importance for the US and world financial markets today, given what is now going on in Japan. For years, as Japan struggled with deflation, beginning in 2016, the Bank of Japan pursued a yield control policy.

Under that policy, the Bank of Japan held its short-term lending rate at a negative level and aimed at keeping the 10-year Japanese government bond rate at zero percent.

Those low interest rates, together with the very much higher interest rates that were on offer in the rest of the world, encouraged global investors to borrow in yen and to finance their investment in other currencies.

Although estimates vary on the size of the Japanese carry trade, it is generally believed that it was at least some $500 billion. That would seem to have provided significant support to the world financial market rally.

The problem for world financial markets today is that at a time when the Federal Reserve and European Central Bank have been cutting interest rates, the Bank of Japan (BOJ) is now in the process of attempting to normalize its monetary policy.

It is being forced to do so both by the increase in Japanese inflation to around three percent, which is above the BOJ’s two percent inflation target, and by the expansionary budget policy now being pursued by Sanae Takaichi, the new Japanese prime minister.

The Bank of Japan is now signaling that later this month it will raise its interest rate at the very time when the Federal Reserve is expected to cut its interest rate next week by another 25 basis points.

The combination of a looser Japanese budget policy and the proposed normalization of the Bank of Japan’s monetary policy has led to a sharp rise in Japanese government bond rates to their highest level in the past twenty years.

Since the start of the year, both the 10-year and the 30-year Japanese government bond yields have increased by 100 basis points to their current levels of around 1.95 percent and 3.95 percent, respectively.

This has considerably increased the relative attractiveness of those bonds with respect to US government bonds, whose yields today are very little different than they were at the start of the year.

The challenge to world financial markets is that the narrowing of the interest rate spread between Japanese bonds and US bonds could lead to an unwinding of the Japanese carry trade and to the repatriation of capital to Japan by that country’s financial institutions.

That process could be reinforced by a meaningful appreciation of the Japanese yen, which is currently estimated to be undervalued by between 15 and 20 percent.

The prospective unwinding of the Japanese carry trade comes at an awkward time for the United States. It comes at the very time when Trump’s large tax cuts have increased the US government’s budget deficit to an annual rate of around $2 trillion.

The last thing that the US government needs is the selling of its bonds by one of its major creditors. That could contribute to a rise in US government bond yields, which could be a trigger for the bursting of the artificial intelligence bubble.

All of this would argue for a rethink of US budget policy with the aim of reducing the budget deficit and our reliance on the kindness of strangers to finance that deficit. Absent such a change, we should brace ourselves for some rough financial market sledding in the year ahead on the back of a meaningful rise in US Treasury bond yields.
